description:
You are working as a database-reliability engineer who has just received a nightly backup-metadata file.  
Your objective is to run a single, **one-liner jq command** that quickly audits the JSON to make sure every backup object contains all four mandatory keys (`id`, `timestamp`, `size`, `status`).  
From that audit you must create a small summary report on disk that other automation can pick up later.

Initial state (already present when the session begins):
1. Directory:  
   /home/user/backups/
2. File:  
   /home/user/backups/metadata.json  
   – a UTF-8 encoded JSON array of backup objects.

Validation rules you must enforce:
• A backup entry is **valid** only if **all four** of the keys `id`, `timestamp`, `size`, `status` are present.  
• Everything else is **invalid**.

What you must produce with a single terminal command:
1. A new file, exactly:  
   /home/user/backups/validation_report.json
2. The file must contain precisely one JSON object, formatted with standard jq “pretty-print” (indentation = 2 spaces).  
   Required keys and their value types:
   • "total_backups"      (integer) → total objects found in metadata.json  
   • "valid_backups"      (integer) → count that passed validation  
   • "invalid_backups"    (integer) → count that failed validation  
   • "invalid_ids"        (array of strings) → list of only the `id` values whose objects were invalid, kept in original order

Example of the exact structure and spacing that automated tests will expect (numbers and IDs will, of course, depend on the file’s real content):

{
  "total_backups": 4,
  "valid_backups": 2,
  "invalid_backups": 2,
  "invalid_ids": [
    "bkp-002",
    "bkp-004"
  ]
}

3. Additionally, append (or create) a plain-text log line in  
   /home/user/backup_validation.log  
   The log must consist of exactly one line ending with a newline:
   Report written to /home/user/backups/validation_report.json

Constraints & tips:
• Solve the whole task with one jq invocation (piping or redirection is fine).  
• Do not mutate /home/user/backups/metadata.json.  
• No root privileges are available.  
• Any intermediate output you echo to the terminal is fine; the automated grading, however, will look only at the two files’ existence and exact contents.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their exact contents:

1. /home/user/backups/metadata.json
[
  {"id":"bkp-001","timestamp":"2023-10-01T02:00:00Z","size":2048,"status":"completed"},
  {"id":"bkp-002","timestamp":"2023-10-02T02:00:00Z","status":"failed"},
  {"id":"bkp-003","timestamp":"2023-10-03T02:00:00Z","size":4096,"status":"completed"},
  {"id":"bkp-004","timestamp":"2023-10-04T02:00:00Z","size":1024}
]

2. Directory /home/user/backups/ has permissions 755.

Artifacts that **must exist** after successful completion:

A. /home/user/backups/validation_report.json
{
  "total_backups": 4,
  "valid_backups": 2,
  "invalid_backups": 2,
  "invalid_ids": [
    "bkp-002",
    "bkp-004"
  ]
}

B. /home/user/backup_validation.log
Report written to /home/user/backups/validation_report.json\n   (a single newline at the end)

Both files must be world-readable (e.g., 644) so the test harness can open them.
